title: The cohomology class associated with a cycle

pages.title: The cohomology class associated with a cycle
pages.html:
4 The class of cohomology associated with a cycle

by A. Grothendieck, written by P. Deligne

This presentation is inspired by notes by Grothendieck, who formed a state 0 of [21]. It defines the cohomology class of a cycle \(X\) in a separate smooth pattern of the finite type on a field and shows that the intersection corresponds to the cup-product.

The Chapter 1 contains some general sorites. 2, the class of a cycle is defined, in several situations more general than the one mentioned above. The main unconsidered compatibility is that between direct image of a cycle and trace morphism in cohomology. 3, the formula of the traces of Lefschetz is deduced from this formalism for an endomorphism with fixed points isolated from a clean and smooth pattern on \(k\) algebraically closed – and for an endomorphism of Frobenius of a curve.

We make the following conditions:

  1. "scheme" means separate noetherian scheme (this is largely a hypothesis of convenience).

  2. In the Chapter 2, an integer is fixed \(n\), and \(n\) is invertible on all the schemes considered.

  3. In the Chapter 3, a prime number is fixed \(\ell\), and \(\ell\) is invertible on all the schemes considered. The cohomology used is always cohomology \(\ell\)-adic:

    \[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X) = \mathbb{Q}_\ell\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}_\ell} \varprojlim \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X,\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n) \text{.} \]

Cycle cohomology classes, trace morphisms, ... are defined by limit passage from the case of finite coefficients \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n\) (cf. [21]).

1 Cohomology with support and cup–products

This paragraph contains reminders of general topology, which the reader is invited to consult only as needed.

1.1 \(H^1\)H1 and torsors

1.1.1

Either \(\mathscr{F}\) an abelian sheaf on a site \(X\). We know that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F})\) classifies \(\mathscr{F}\)-torsors on \(X\). We will normalize (\(=\)choose the sign) of isomorphism \((\)all classes of isomorphism of \(F\)-tors\()\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F})\) such that for any exact sequence \(0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}\xrightarrow{\alpha}\mathscr{G}\xrightarrow{\beta}\mathscr{H}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\) and all \(h\in\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,\mathscr{H})\), the \(\mathscr{F}\)-torseur \(\beta^{-1}(h)\subset \mathscr{G}\), on which \(F\) acts by \((f,x)\mapsto \alpha(f)+x\), or class \(\partial h\).

1.1.2

Either \(\mathscr{P}\) a \(\mathscr{F}\)-torseur. If \((U_i)\) is an open cover of \(X\), and \(p_i\) a section of \(\mathscr{P}\) on \(U_i\), the Čech cocycle is associated with \(\mathscr{P}\)

\[ p_{i j} = p_j - p_i \qquad \text{(} p_{i j}\in \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(U_i\times U_j,\mathscr{F}) \text{).} \]

If, according to the usual rule, the application is defined \(\operatorname{\check H}^\bullet(X,\mathscr{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X,\mathscr{F})\) in such a way that it is a morphism of \(\delta\)-fonctors, the image of \((p_{i j})\in \operatorname{\check H}^1(X,\mathscr{F})\) in \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F})\) is the class of \(\mathscr{P}\), as normalized by 1.1.1.

1.1.3

The definition of \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X,\mathscr{F})\) is as follows: for \(\mathscr{F}^\bullet\) a resolution with acyclic components of \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i\Gamma(X,\mathscr{F}^\bullet)\). The structure of \(\delta\)-fonctor is obtained by combining with a short exact sequence of sheaves a short exact sequence of resolutions that remains accurate after application of the \(\Gamma\). If \(\mathscr{F}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(\mathscr{F}\), the connection homomorphism \(\partial\) associated with

\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \mathscr{F} \ar[r] & \mathscr{F}^0 \ar[r]^-d & \ker(d) \ar[r] & 0 } \]

induces the opposite of definition isomorphism \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X,\mathscr{F}) = \Gamma\left(X,\ker(d)\right)/d \Gamma(X,\mathscr{F}^0)\).

1.1.4

Either \(U\) an open part of \(X\) (a subbeam of the final sheaf) or \(D\) the "additional closed." It is known that \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_D^1(X,\mathscr{F})\) classifies \(\mathscr{F}\)-torsors on \(X\), trivialised on \(U\). For any short exact sequence \(0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{F}\xrightarrow{\alpha} \mathscr{G}\xrightarrow{\beta}\mathscr{H}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\), and any supporting section in \(D\) \(h\in\underline{\operatorname{H}}_D^0(X,\mathscr{H})\), the torsor \(\beta^{-1}(h)\), trivialised on \(U\) by the section \(0\), has for class \(\partial h\).

The long exact sequence of supporting cohomology

\[ \xymatrix{ \cdots \ar[r]^-\partial & \underline{\operatorname{H}}_D^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X,\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(U,\mathscr{F}) \ar[r]^-\partial & \cdots } \]

is defined from the sequence of searchers

\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \Gamma_D \ar[r] & \Gamma \ar[r] & \Gamma(U,-) \ar[r] & 0 } \]

For any section \(f\in \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(U,\mathscr{F})\), \(\partial f\in \underline{\operatorname{H}}_D^1(X,\mathscr{F})\) is the class of the trivial torsor \(\mathscr{F}\), trivialised on \(U\) by \(f\).

1.1.5

Either \(j:U\hookrightarrow X\). If \(\mathscr{F}\) is injected into \(j_* j^* \mathscr{F}\), the exact sequence

\[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & \mathscr{F} \ar[r] & j_* j^* \mathscr{F} \ar[r] & j_* j^* \mathscr{F} / \mathscr{F} \ar[r] & 0 } \]

provides \(\partial:\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(j_* j^*\mathscr{F}/\mathscr{F}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}_D^0(j_* j^* \mathscr{F}/\mathscr{F}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_D^1(X,\mathscr{F})\). The compound application

\[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(U,\mathscr{F}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,j_* j^*\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,j_* j^*\mathscr{F} / \mathscr{F}) \ar[r]^-\partial & \underline{\operatorname{H}}_D^1(X,\mathscr{F}) } \]

is the opposite of the application considered in 1.1.4.

1.1.6

It is recalled that if \(\mathscr{L}\) is an invertible sheaf on a diagram \(X\), the corresponding \(\mathbb{G}_m\)-torsor is the sheaf \(\operatorname{Isom}(\mathscr{O},\mathscr{L})\), on which \(\mathbb{G}_m\) acts by \((\lambda,f)\mapsto f\circ (\lambda\cdot) = \lambda f\). It is also recalled that if \(D\) is a partitioner of Cartier on \(X\), and \(j:U\hookrightarrow X\) the inclusion of additional aperture, the invertible sheaf \(\mathscr{O}(D)\) is the subbeam of \(j_*\mathscr{O}_U\) formed of local sections \(s\) such as \(s f\) be in \(\mathscr{O}_X\), for \(f\) a local equation of \(D\).

1.2 Cup-products

In this issue, we develop some remarks on cup-products in support cohomology that we will reserve, in [V], to connect the duality of Poincaré curves and self-duality of the jacobian.

1.2.1

Be \(X\) a site, \(Y\) a closed part of \(X\), and \(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}\) two abelian sheaves on \(X\). For example: \(X\) a scheme, \(Y\) a closed subschema and \(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}\) sheaves on \({X}_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}\). Set a product

\[ \tag{1.2.1.1} \Gamma_Y(X,\mathscr{F})\otimes \Gamma(Y,\mathscr{G}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma_Y(X,\mathscr{F}\otimes\mathscr{G}) \text{.} \]

The product of a section \(s\), supported in \(Y\), of \(\mathscr{F}\) by a section \(t\) of \(\mathscr{G}\) on \(Y\) is obtained as follows: locally on \(X\), \(t\) is the restriction to \(Y\) of a section \(t'\) of \(\mathscr{G}\) on \(X\), and the product is formed \(s\otimes t'\). It is supported in \(Y\), and does not depend on the choice of \(t'\), which legitimizes and allows the globalization of the definition.

Be \(i:Y\hookrightarrow X\) the inclusion morphism.1.2.1.1) is the product.

\[ \tag{1.2.1.2} i^!\mathscr{F}\otimes i^*\mathscr{G} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i^!(\mathscr{F}\otimes\mathscr{G}) \text{,} \]

of which (1.2.1.1) is deducted by application of \(\Gamma(Y,-)\).

1.2.2

Derive these arrows. Be \(\mathscr{K}\), \(\mathscr{L}\) and \(\mathscr{M}\) in the derived category, and a bilinear application \(\mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\mathscr{L}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{M}\). By derivation of (1.2.1.1), it is deduced from

\[ \tag{1.2.2.1} \mathsf{R}\Gamma_Y(X,\mathscr{K}) \overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathsf{R}\Gamma(Y,\mathscr{L}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R}\Gamma_Y(X,\mathscr{M}) \]

Inducing

\[ \tag{1.2.2.2} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_Y^i(X,\mathscr{K})\otimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}^j(Y,\mathscr{L}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_Y^{i+j}(X,\mathscr{M}) \]

(the cup-product). The local arrow (1.2.1.2) provides

\[ \tag{1.2.2.3} \mathsf{R} i^! \mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} i^*\mathscr{L} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R} i^!\mathscr{M} \text{,} \]

of which (1.2.2.1) is deducted by application of \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma(Y,-)\).

1.2.3

Above, I have ignored the conflicts that the use of the same formula of diversions on the right (\(\mathsf{R}\Gamma\)) and on the left (\(\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\)).

  1. At the next issue, the right derivatives considered will all be of finished cohomological dimension. This allows to work systematically in the derived categories \(\mathsf{D}^-\). To have complexes both flat and flaccid, we use canonical flaccid resolutions as in [2].

  2. For a more general theory, it ceases to be to interpret the bilinear applications of \(\mathscr{K}\) and \(\mathscr{L}\) in \(\mathscr{M}\) as morphisms of \(\mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\mathscr{L}\) in \(\mathscr{M}\). For example, \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_Y(X,\mathscr{K})\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\mathsf{R}\Gamma(Y,\mathscr{L})\) is not defined if both factors are in \(\mathsf{D}^+\). One solution is to work in \(\mathsf{D}^+\), to define

    \[ \operatorname{Bil}(\mathscr{K},\mathscr{L};\mathscr{M}) = \varinjlim \hom(\mathscr{K}'\otimes\mathscr{L}',\mathscr{M}') \text{,} \]

    where the limit is taken on quasi-isomorphisms \(\mathscr{K}'\xrightarrow{\sim} \mathscr{K}\), \(\mathscr{L}'\xrightarrow{\sim} \mathscr{L}\), \(\mathscr{M}\xrightarrow{\sim} \mathscr{M}'\) (\(\mathscr{K}'\), \(\mathscr{L}'\), \(\mathscr{M}'\) lower bounded) and where \(\hom\) is for "complex morphism, close to homotopy," and to systematically use such bilinear applications, without ever mentioning \(\otimes\).

1.2.4 Second theme

Be \(U\) an open \(X\) and \(j:U\hookrightarrow X\) the inclusion morphism. For \(\mathscr{K}\) in the derived category, on \(U\), we pose \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_!(U,\mathscr{K})=\mathsf{R}\Gamma(X,j_!\mathscr{K})\). For \(\mathscr{K}\), \(\mathscr{L}\) and \(\mathscr{M}\) on \(U\), and a bilinear application \(\mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\mathscr{L}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{M}\), we want to define

\[ \tag{1.2.4.1} \mathsf{R}\Gamma(U,\mathscr{K})\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\mathsf{R}\Gamma_!(U,\mathscr{L})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathsf{R}\Gamma_!(U,\mathscr{M}) \text{.} \]

At the level of the sheaves, and of their overall sections, such a product is deduced from the isomorphism \(j_*\mathscr{F}\otimes j_!\mathscr{G}\xleftarrow{\sim} j_!(\mathscr{F}\otimes \mathscr{G})\), but care must be taken that \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_!\) is not in general the derivative of the actuator \(\Gamma(X,j_!-)\).

Let's start by defining

\[ \xymatrix{ \mathsf{R} j_* \mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} j_! \mathscr{L} & j_!(\mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathscr{L}) \ar[r] \ar[l]_-\sim& j_!\mathscr{M} . } \]

Applying \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma(X,-)\), we find

\[ \xymatrix{ \mathsf{R}\Gamma(U,\mathscr{K})\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\mathsf{R}\Gamma_!(U,\mathscr{L}) = \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X,\mathsf{R} j_*\mathscr{K})\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X,j_!\mathscr{L}) \ar[r] & \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X,j_! \mathscr{M}) . } \]

Again, the conflict between the left and the right will resolve the next number by working in \(\mathsf{D}^-\).

1.2.5 Coda

Been \(j:U\hookrightarrow X\) an open part and \(i:Y\hookrightarrow U\) a closed part of \(U\). Either \(\overline{Y}\) a closed \(X\) such as \(\overline{Y}\cap U=Y\), (e.g. the addition of \(U\setminus Y\)) Either \(\mathscr{K}\) on \(U\)We're laying down. \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_{Y!}(U,\mathscr{K})=\mathsf{R}\Gamma_!(Y,\mathsf{R} i^!\mathscr{K})\) where \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_!\) is related to the inclusion of \(Y\) in \(\overline{Y}\). For all open \(V\) of the \(U\), containing \(Y\), \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_{Y!}(U,\mathscr{K})\) sent in \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_!(V,\mathscr{K})\): if we still note \(i\) the inclusion of \(\overline{Y}\) in \(X\), \(j\) that of \(Y\) in \(\overline{Y}\) and \(k\) that of \(V\) in \(X\), we have \(\mathsf{R} i^!\mathscr{K} = j^*\mathsf{R} i^! k_!(k^*\mathscr{K})\), hence a morphism \(j_!\mathsf{R} i^!\mathscr{K} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R} i^! k_!(k^*\mathscr{K})\)Applying it \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma(\overline{Y},-)\), we find \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_{Y!}(U,\mathscr{K})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R}\Gamma_{\overline{Y}}(k_! k^* \mathscr{K}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R}\Gamma(k_! k^*\mathscr{K}) = \mathsf{R}\Gamma_!(V,\mathscr{K})\).

For \(\mathscr{K},\mathscr{L},\mathscr{M}\) on \(U\), and a bilinear application \(\mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\mathscr{L}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{M}\), we want to define

\[ \tag{1.2.5.1} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^n(U,\mathscr{K})\otimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}_!^m(Y,\mathscr{L}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_{Y!}^{n+m}(U,\mathscr{M}) \]

(the latter group itself sends itself to \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_!^{n+m}(V,\mathscr{M})\)). In the derived category, it is necessary to define

\[ \tag{1.2.5.2} \mathsf{R}\Gamma_Y(U,\mathscr{K}) \overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathsf{R}\Gamma_!(R,\mathscr{L}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R}\Gamma_{Y!}(U,\mathscr{M}) \text{.} \]

We identify \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_Y\) to \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma(Y,\mathsf{R} i^!\mathscr{K})\). The product we are looking for is of the type (1.2.4.1) relating to the local product (1.2.2.3) on \(Y\): \(\mathsf{R} i^! \mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\mathscr{L} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R} i^! \mathscr{M}\).

1.2.6

Above, the absolute sorite was carried out. There is a parallel relative sorite, with \(\Gamma\) replaced by \(f_*\) by \(f\) a morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\).

1.3 The Koszul Rule

Be \(A\) a commutative ring, and \((V_i)_{i\in I}\) a finished family of \(A\)-graded modules (or \(\mathbb{Z}/2\)-graded). Recall the definition of the graduated tensor product \(\bigotimes_{i\in I} V_i\), within the meaning of the Koszul rule (cf. [2]) For each total order \(a\) on \(I\), we will define a module \(V(a)\). We will also define a transitive system of isomorphisms \(\varphi_{a b}:V(b)\xrightarrow{\sim} V(a)\) and the graduated tensorial product of \(V_i\) will be the "common value" \(\varprojlim V(a)\) of this module system.

  1. \(V(a) = \bigotimes_{i\in I} |V_i|\) (ordinary tensor product of ungraded modules underlying \(V_i\)).

  2. If the \(x_i\in V_i\) are homogeneous, we take \(\varphi_{a b}(\otimes x_i) = (-1)^N \otimes x_i\), where \(N\) is the sum of \(\deg(x_i)\deg(x_j)\) extended to couples \((i,j)\) such as \(i<_a j\) and \(i>_b j\).

Example 1

Take \(I=\{1,2\}\) and be \(a\) the order where \(1<2\), \(b\) the order where \(1>2\). Be \(v_i\in V_i\), homogeneous, and note \(v_1\otimes v_2\) (rep. \(v_2\otimes v_1\)) the image in the tensor product graduated from the product of \(v_i\) in \(V(a)\) (rep. \(V(b)\)).

\[ v_1\otimes v_2 = (-1)^{\deg(v_1)\deg(v_2)} v_2\otimes v_1 \]

(Koszul rule).

Example 2

If the \(V_i\) are all of degree \(1\), we have , linking the underlying module to the graduated tensor product, and the ordinary tensor product of the modules \(|V_i|\) underlying \(V_i\), a canonical isomorphism

\[ \left|\bigotimes V_i\right| \simeq \bigotimes |V_i|\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\bigwedge^{|I|}\mathbb{Z}^I \text{.} \]

Example 3

For \(A\) a field, and \(X_i\) a finite family of spaces, the formula of Künneth is written \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet\left(\prod X_i,A\right) = \bigotimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_i,A)\).

Either \(V^\vee\) the graduated dual of \(V\). The canonical application

\[ \tag{1.3.1} V^\vee\otimes V = V\otimes V^\vee \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A \]

is \(v'\otimes v\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} v'(v)\).

It is now assumed that \(A\) is a field, and only vector spaces of finite dimension are considered. Canon isomorphism

\[ \tag{1.3.2} W\otimes V^\vee \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \hom(V,W) \]

is \(w\otimes v'\mapsto \left(v\mapsto w\cdot v'(v)\right)\). Through this isomorphism, the composition \(\hom(Y,Z)\otimes \hom(X,Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \hom(X,Z)\) is identified with the morphism induced by (1.3.1): \(Z\otimes Y^\vee\otimes Y\otimes X^\vee \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\otimes X^\vee\).

The trace of \(f:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} V^\vee\) (nil for \(f\) homogeneity of degree \(\ne 0\)) is the image of \(f\) by

\[ \tag{1.3.4} \operatorname{tr}:\hom(V,V) {\xleftarrow{\sim}}_\text{(1.3.2)} V\otimes V^\vee = V^\vee \otimes V \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}_\text{(1.3.1)} A . \]

It is easily verified that for \(f\) degree \(0\),

\[ \tag{1.3.5} \operatorname{tr}(f,V) = \sum (-1)^i \operatorname{tr}(f,V^i) \text{.} \]

If we express that the two morphisms composed of morphisms (1.3.1) \(V^\vee\otimes V\otimes W^\vee\otimes W\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k\) switch, we find that for \(f:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} W\) and \(g:W\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} V\) homogeneous, we have

\[ \tag{1.3.6} \operatorname{tr}(f g) = (-1)^{\deg(f)\deg(g)} \operatorname{tr}(g f) \text{.} \]

\[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(U,\mathscr{F}) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,j_* j^*\mathscr{F}) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X,j_* j^*\mathscr{F} / \mathscr{F}) \ar[r]^-\partial & \underline{\operatorname{H}}_D^1(X,\mathscr{F}) } \]
is the opposite of the application considered in 1.1.4.

It is recalled that if \(\mathscr{L}\) is an invertible sheaf on a diagram \(X\), the corresponding \(\mathbb{G}_m\)-torsor is the sheaf \(\operatorname{Isom}(\mathscr{O},\mathscr{L})\), on which \(\mathbb{G}_m\) acts by \((\lambda,f)\mapsto f\circ (\lambda\cdot) = \lambda f\). It is also recalled that if \(D\) is a partitioner of Cartier on \(X\), and \(j:U\hookrightarrow X\) the inclusion of additional aperture, the invertible sheaf \(\mathscr{O}(D)\) is the subbeam of \(j_*\mathscr{O}_U\) formed of local sections \(s\) such as \(s f\) be in \(\mathscr{O}_X\), for \(f\) a local equation of \(D\).
1.2 Cup-products
In this issue, we develop some remarks on cup-products in support cohomology that we will reserve, in [V], to connect the duality of Poincaré curves and self-duality of the jacobian.

Be \(X\) a site, \(Y\) a closed part of \(X\), and \(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}\) two abelian sheaves on \(X\). For example: \(X\) a scheme, \(Y\) a closed subschema and \(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}\) sheaves on \({X}_{\mathrm{\acute{e}t}}\). Set a product
\[ \tag{1.2.1.1} \Gamma_Y(X,\mathscr{F})\otimes \Gamma(Y,\mathscr{G}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma_Y(X,\mathscr{F}\otimes\mathscr{G}) \text{.} \]

The product of a section \(s\), supported in \(Y\), of \(\mathscr{F}\) by a section \(t\) of \(\mathscr{G}\) on \(Y\) is obtained as follows: locally on \(X\), \(t\) is the restriction to \(Y\) of a section \(t'\) of \(\mathscr{G}\) on \(X\), and the product is formed \(s\otimes t'\). It is supported in \(Y\), and does not depend on the choice of \(t'\), which legitimizes and allows the globalization of the definition.
Be \(i:Y\hookrightarrow X\) the inclusion morphism.1.2.1.1) is the product.
\[ \tag{1.2.1.2} i^!\mathscr{F}\otimes i^*\mathscr{G} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i^!(\mathscr{F}\otimes\mathscr{G}) \text{,} \]
of which (1.2.1.1) is deducted by application of \(\Gamma(Y,-)\).

Derive these arrows. Be \(\mathscr{K}\), \(\mathscr{L}\) and \(\mathscr{M}\) in the derived category, and a bilinear application \(\mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\mathscr{L}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{M}\). By derivation of (1.2.1.1), it is deduced from

\[ \tag{1.2.2.1} \mathsf{R}\Gamma_Y(X,\mathscr{K}) \overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathsf{R}\Gamma(Y,\mathscr{L}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R}\Gamma_Y(X,\mathscr{M}) \]
Inducing

\[ \tag{1.2.2.2} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_Y^i(X,\mathscr{K})\otimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}^j(Y,\mathscr{L}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_Y^{i+j}(X,\mathscr{M}) \]
(the cup-product). The local arrow (1.2.1.2) provides
\[ \tag{1.2.2.3} \mathsf{R} i^! \mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} i^*\mathscr{L} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R} i^!\mathscr{M} \text{,} \]
of which (1.2.2.1) is deducted by application of \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma(Y,-)\).
Above, I have ignored the conflicts that the use of the same formula of diversions on the right (\(\mathsf{R}\Gamma\)) and on the left (\(\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\)).

1. At the next issue, the right derivatives considered will all be of finished cohomological dimension. This allows to work systematically in the derived categories \(\mathsf{D}^-\). To have complexes both flat and flaccid, we use canonical flaccid resolutions as in [2].

  2. For a more general theory, it ceases to be to interpret the bilinear applications of \(\mathscr{K}\) and \(\mathscr{L}\) in \(\mathscr{M}\) as morphisms of \(\mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\mathscr{L}\) in \(\mathscr{M}\). For example, \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_Y(X,\mathscr{K})\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\mathsf{R}\Gamma(Y,\mathscr{L})\) is not defined if both factors are in \(\mathsf{D}^+\). One solution is to work in \(\mathsf{D}^+\), to define

    \[ \operatorname{Bil}(\mathscr{K},\mathscr{L};\mathscr{M}) = \varinjlim \hom(\mathscr{K}'\otimes\mathscr{L}',\mathscr{M}') \text{,} \]

    where the limit is taken on quasi-isomorphisms \(\mathscr{K}'\xrightarrow{\sim} \mathscr{K}\), \(\mathscr{L}'\xrightarrow{\sim} \mathscr{L}\), \(\mathscr{M}\xrightarrow{\sim} \mathscr{M}'\) (\(\mathscr{K}'\), \(\mathscr{L}'\), \(\mathscr{M}'\) lower bounded) and where \(\hom\) is for "complex morphism, close to homotopy," and to systematically use such bilinear applications, without ever mentioning \(\otimes\).
1.2.4 Second theme

Be \(U\) an open \(X\) and \(j:U\hookrightarrow X\) the inclusion morphism. For \(\mathscr{K}\) in the derived category, on \(U\), we pose \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_!(U,\mathscr{K})=\mathsf{R}\Gamma(X,j_!\mathscr{K})\). For \(\mathscr{K}\), \(\mathscr{L}\) and \(\mathscr{M}\) on \(U\), and a bilinear application \(\mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\mathscr{L}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{M}\), we want to define

\[ \tag{1.2.4.1} \mathsf{R}\Gamma(U,\mathscr{K})\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\mathsf{R}\Gamma_!(U,\mathscr{L})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathsf{R}\Gamma_!(U,\mathscr{M}) \text{.} \]

At the level of the sheaves, and of their overall sections, such a product is deduced from the isomorphism \(j_*\mathscr{F}\otimes j_!\mathscr{G}\xleftarrow{\sim} j_!(\mathscr{F}\otimes \mathscr{G})\), but care must be taken that \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_!\) is not in general the derivative of the actuator \(\Gamma(X,j_!-)\).
Let's start by defining
\[ \xymatrix{ \mathsf{R} j_* \mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} j_! \mathscr{L} & j_!(\mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathscr{L}) \ar[r] \ar[l]_-\sim& j_!\mathscr{M} . } \]
Applying \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma(X,-)\), we find

\[ \xymatrix{ \mathsf{R}\Gamma(U,\mathscr{K})\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\mathsf{R}\Gamma_!(U,\mathscr{L}) = \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X,\mathsf{R} j_*\mathscr{K})\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X,j_!\mathscr{L}) \ar[r] & \mathsf{R}\Gamma(X,j_! \mathscr{M}) . } \]
Again, the conflict between the left and the right will resolve the next number by working in \(\mathsf{D}^-\).

Been \(j:U\hookrightarrow X\) an open part and \(i:Y\hookrightarrow U\) a closed part of \(U\). Either \(\overline{Y}\) a closed \(X\) such as \(\overline{Y}\cap U=Y\), (e.g. the addition of \(U\setminus Y\)) Either \(\mathscr{K}\) on \(U\)We're laying down. \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_{Y!}(U,\mathscr{K})=\mathsf{R}\Gamma_!(Y,\mathsf{R} i^!\mathscr{K})\) where \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_!\) is related to the inclusion of \(Y\) in \(\overline{Y}\). For all open \(V\) of the \(U\), containing \(Y\), \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_{Y!}(U,\mathscr{K})\) sent in \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_!(V,\mathscr{K})\): if we still note \(i\) the inclusion of \(\overline{Y}\) in \(X\), \(j\) that of \(Y\) in \(\overline{Y}\) and \(k\) that of \(V\) in \(X\), we have \(\mathsf{R} i^!\mathscr{K} = j^*\mathsf{R} i^! k_!(k^*\mathscr{K})\), hence a morphism \(j_!\mathsf{R} i^!\mathscr{K} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R} i^! k_!(k^*\mathscr{K})\)Applying it \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma(\overline{Y},-)\), we find \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_{Y!}(U,\mathscr{K})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R}\Gamma_{\overline{Y}}(k_! k^* \mathscr{K}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R}\Gamma(k_! k^*\mathscr{K}) = \mathsf{R}\Gamma_!(V,\mathscr{K})\).

For \(\mathscr{K},\mathscr{L},\mathscr{M}\) on \(U\), and a bilinear application \(\mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\mathscr{L}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{M}\), we want to define

\[ \tag{1.2.5.1} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^n(U,\mathscr{K})\otimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}_!^m(Y,\mathscr{L}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_{Y!}^{n+m}(U,\mathscr{M}) \]
(the latter group itself sends itself to \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_!^{n+m}(V,\mathscr{M})\)). In the derived category, it is necessary to define

\[ \tag{1.2.5.2} \mathsf{R}\Gamma_Y(U,\mathscr{K}) \overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes} \mathsf{R}\Gamma_!(R,\mathscr{L}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R}\Gamma_{Y!}(U,\mathscr{M}) \text{.} \]

We identify \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma_Y\) to \(\mathsf{R}\Gamma(Y,\mathsf{R} i^!\mathscr{K})\). The product we are looking for is of the type (1.2.4.1) relating to the local product (1.2.2.3) on \(Y\): \(\mathsf{R} i^! \mathscr{K}\overset{\mathsf{L}}{\otimes}\mathscr{L} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathsf{R} i^! \mathscr{M}\).

Above, the absolute sorite was carried out. There is a parallel relative sorite, with \(\Gamma\) replaced by \(f_*\) by \(f\) a morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\).
1.3 The Koszul Rule

Be \(A\) a commutative ring, and \((V_i)_{i\in I}\) a finished family of \(A\)-graded modules (or \(\mathbb{Z}/2\)-graded). Recall the definition of the graduated tensor product \(\bigotimes_{i\in I} V_i\), within the meaning of the Koszul rule (cf. [2]) For each total order \(a\) on \(I\), we will define a module \(V(a)\). We will also define a transitive system of isomorphisms \(\varphi_{a b}:V(b)\xrightarrow{\sim} V(a)\) and the graduated tensorial product of \(V_i\) will be the "common value" \(\varprojlim V(a)\) of this module system.

1. \(V(a) = \bigotimes_{i\in I} |V_i|\) (ordinary tensor product of ungraded modules underlying \(V_i\)).

  2. If the \(x_i\in V_i\) are homogeneous, we take \(\varphi_{a b}(\otimes x_i) = (-1)^N \otimes x_i\), where \(N\) is the sum of \(\deg(x_i)\deg(x_j)\) extended to couples \((i,j)\) such as \(i<_a j\) and \(i>_b j\).
Example 1

Take \(I=\{1,2\}\) and be \(a\) the order where \(1<2\), \(b\) the order where \(1>2\). Be \(v_i\in V_i\), homogeneous, and note \(v_1\otimes v_2\) (rep. \(v_2\otimes v_1\)) the image in the tensor product graduated from the product of \(v_i\) in \(V(a)\) (rep. \(V(b)\)).
\[ v_1\otimes v_2 = (-1)^{\deg(v_1)\deg(v_2)} v_2\otimes v_1 \]
(Koszul rule).
Example 2

If the \(V_i\) are all of degree \(1\), we have , linking the underlying module to the graduated tensor product, and the ordinary tensor product of the modules \(|V_i|\) underlying \(V_i\), a canonical isomorphism
\[ \left|\bigotimes V_i\right| \simeq \bigotimes |V_i|\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\bigwedge^{|I|}\mathbb{Z}^I \text{.} \]
Example 3

For \(A\) a field, and \(X_i\) a finite family of spaces, the formula of Künneth is written \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet\left(\prod X_i,A\right) = \bigotimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X_i,A)\).
Either \(V^\vee\) the graduated dual of \(V\). The canonical application
\[ \tag{1.3.1} V^\vee\otimes V = V\otimes V^\vee \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A \]
is \(v'\otimes v\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} v'(v)\).
It is now assumed that \(A\) is a field, and only vector spaces of finite dimension are considered. Canon isomorphism
\[ \tag{1.3.2} W\otimes V^\vee \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \hom(V,W) \]

is \(w\otimes v'\mapsto \left(v\mapsto w\cdot v'(v)\right)\). Through this isomorphism, the composition \(\hom(Y,Z)\otimes \hom(X,Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \hom(X,Z)\) is identified with the morphism induced by (1.3.1): \(Z\otimes Y^\vee\otimes Y\otimes X^\vee \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\otimes X^\vee\).
The trace of \(f:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} V^\vee\) (nil for \(f\) homogeneity of degree \(\ne 0\)) is the image of \(f\) by

\[ \tag{1.3.4} \operatorname{tr}:\hom(V,V) {\xleftarrow{\sim}}_\text{(1.3.2)} V\otimes V^\vee = V^\vee \otimes V \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}_\text{(1.3.1)} A . \]
It is easily verified that for \(f\) degree \(0\),
\[ \tag{1.3.5} \operatorname{tr}(f,V) = \sum (-1)^i \operatorname{tr}(f,V^i) \text{.} \]

If we express that the two morphisms composed of morphisms (1.3.1) \(V^\vee\otimes V\otimes W^\vee\otimes W\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k\) switch, we find that for \(f:V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} W\) and \(g:W\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} V\) homogeneous, we have
\[ \tag{1.3.6} \operatorname{tr}(f g) = (-1)^{\deg(f)\deg(g)} \operatorname{tr}(g f) \text{.} \]

2 The class of cohomology associated with a cycle

The class of a divisor

2.1.1

Be \(D\) a partitioner of Cartier in a scheme \(X\). Outside \(D\), the invertible sheaf \(\mathscr{O}(D)\) is triviatized by the section \(1\). The class \(\operatorname{cl}(D)\) of \(D\), in \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_D^1(X,\mathbb{G}_m)\), is the class of \(\mathbb{G}_m\)-triviatizer on \(X\setminus D\) correspondent (1.1.6 and 1.1.4).

Either \(\partial:\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X\setminus D,\mathbb{G}_m)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_D^i(X,\mathbb{G}_m)\) morphism 1.1.4. If \(D\) admits a global equation \(f\), the multiplication by \(f\) is an isomorphism of \(\mathscr{O}(D)\), trivialised by \(1\) on \(X\setminus D\), with \(\mathscr{O}\), trivialised by \(f\) on \(X\setminus D\). 1.1.4, so we've got

\[ \tag{2.1.1} \operatorname{cl}(D) = \partial f \text{.} \]

For any morphism \(u:X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) such that \(u^* D\) is still a divisor of Cartier (i.e., \(u^{-1} D\) disjointed from \(\operatorname{Ass}(X')\)), we have \(\operatorname{cl}(u^* D) = u^*\operatorname{cl}(D)\). If we wanted such a functionality for any morphism \(u\), we should consider not Cartier divisors, but more generally invertible sheaves with a section.

Remember that the entire \(n\) is now assumed to be invertible on the schemes considered. Either \(\partial:\underline{\operatorname{H}}_D^i(X,\mathbb{G}_m) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_D^{i+1}(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\) the starboard for the exact continuation of Kummer \(0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \boldsymbol{\mu}_n \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{G}_m \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{G}_m \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0\).

Definition 2.1.2

The class \(\operatorname{cl}_n(D)\) of \(D\) in \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_D^2(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\) is \(\partial \operatorname{cl}(D)\).

When there is no risk of confusion, the mention of \(n\) will be omitted.

2.1.3

The diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0(X\setminus D,\mathbb{G}_m) \ar[r]^-\partial \ar[d]^-\partial & \underline{\operatorname{H}}_D^1(X,\mathbb{G}_m) \ar[d]^-\partial \\ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X\setminus D,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \ar[r]^-\partial & \underline{\operatorname{H}}_D^2(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) } \]

If \(D\) admits a global equation \(f\), \(\operatorname{cl}_n(D)\) is therefore the opposite of the image by \(\partial\) of the class in \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^1(X\setminus D,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\) of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\)-torseur of the roots \(n\)-ths of \(f\).

Proposition 2.1.4

Either \(i\) the inclusion of \(D\) in \(X\). If \(D\) and \(X\) are regular, the supporting cohomology sheaves \(\mathsf{R}^p i^! \boldsymbol{\mu}_n\) are void for \(p=0,2\), and \(\mathsf{R}^2 i^!\boldsymbol{\mu}_n = \underline{\mathbb{Z}/n}\), generated by \(\operatorname{cl}_n(D)\).

It is sufficient to prove that, for \(X\) strictly local and \(D\) defined by a regular parameter, we have \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_D^p(X,d\mu_n) = 0\) for \(p=0,1\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^2(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) = \mathbb{Z}/n\) generated by \(\operatorname{cl}(D)\). Noting by \(\sim\) reduced cohomology, we have \(\widetilde{\underline{\operatorname{H}}}^{p-1}(X\setminus D,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_D^p(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n)\). The assertion for \(p=0,1\) expresses that \(D\) does not disconnect by \(X\), and for \(p=2\) results, via 2.1.3, Abhyankar's lemma.

This is a partial analogue of the relative theorem ([I]Etal Cohomology, 5.3.4) Grothendieck conjectures that the \(\mathsf{R}^p i^! \boldsymbol{\mu}_n\) are null for \(p\ne 2\), at least for \(X\) excellent (purity guess), but this is known only in characteristic \(0\) ([2]).

Theorem 2.1.5 (Basic compatibility)

Be \(X\) uen smooth curve on an algebraically closed field \(k\), \(P\) a closed point of \(X\) and \(\operatorname{tr}\) the compound \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_P^2(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^2(X,\boldsymbol{\mu}_n) \xrightarrow{\operatorname{tr}} \mathbb{Z}/n\).

\[ \operatorname{tr} \operatorname{cl}(P) = 1 \text{.} \]

Either \(\overline{X}\) the projective and smooth curve complementing \(X\). The formula expresses that the invertible sheaf \(\mathscr{O}(P)\) on \(\overline{X}\) is of degree \(1\).

2.2 Cohomological method

2.2.1

Either \(X\) a diagram (noetherian). Let's remember that a sub-scheme \(Y\) of the \(X\) is said to be full local intersection, co-dimension \(c\), if, locally (on \(Y\)), it is defined by a regular sequence of \(c\) equations in \(X\). For \(X\) the spectrum of a local ring \(A\) maximum ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\) and \(Y\) ideal \(\mathfrak{a}\), it means that \(\exp^i(A/\mathfrak{a},A) = 0\) for \(i<\dim(\mathfrak{a}/\mathfrak{m}\mathfrak{a})=c\), and any sequence of elements of \(\mathfrak{a}\), image in \(\mathfrak{a}/\mathfrak{m}\mathfrak{a}\) a base of \(\mathfrak{a}/\mathfrak{m}\mathfrak{a}\), is a regular sequence of equations for \(Y\).

2.2.2

Either \(i:Y\hookrightarrow X\) full local intersection, co-dimension \(c\). It is proposed to define a local fundamental class \(\operatorname{cl}(Y)\) which is a global section of the support cohomology sheaf \(\mathsf{R}^{2c}i^! \mathbb{Z}/n(c)\) (remember that \(\mathbb{Z}/n(c)=\boldsymbol{\mu}_n^{\otimes c}\)).

Locally, \(Y\) is the intersection of a sequence of \(c\) divisors \(D_i\), and we define \(\operatorname{cl}(Y)\) as the cup-product of \(\operatorname{cl}(D_i)\). Each \(\operatorname{cl}(D_i)\) is supported in \(D_i\), their product is supported in \(Y\). That, locally, this product does not depend on the choice of \(D_i\), results from 2.2.3 below and the following invariance properties:

  1. localization compatibility;

  2. independence of the order of \(D_i\) (the \(\operatorname{cl}(D_i)\) are of degree \(2\), even, so the cup-product is commutative);

  3. the product depends only on the "flag" \(D_1\supset D_1\cap D_2\supset \cdots\supset Y\).

In order to prove c, the existence of a product (variant of 1.2.1)

\[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_{D_1}^\bullet(X)\otimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}_{D_1\cap D_2}^\bullet(D_1)\otimes \cdots \otimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}_Y^\bullet(D_1\cap \cdots \cap D_{c-1}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}_Y^\bullet(X) \text{;} \]

the product of \(\operatorname{cl}(D_i)\) is still the product of (\(\operatorname{cl}(D_i)\) restricted to \(D_1\cap \cdots \cap D_{i-1})=(\operatorname{cl}(D_1\cap \cdots \cap D_i)\) in \(D_1\cap \cdots \cap D_{i-1})\).

Lemma 2.2.3

Be \(A\) a local ring of maximum ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\) and \(u=(u_1,\dots,u_c)\), \(v=(v_1,\dots,v_c)\) two regular suites generating the same ideal \(\mathfrak{a}\). There is then a suite \(w_i\) (\(1\leqslant i\leqslant N\)) of such suites, connecting them, such as \(w_{i+1}\) deduces itself from \(w_i\) by a permutation, or by changing only the last element.

Since permutations are available, it would be the same to afford to change a single element, rather than the last. 2.2.1In the vector space \(\mathfrak{a}/\mathfrak{m}\mathfrak{a}\), we can move from one base to another through a series of permutations and basic changes that change only one vector. The linear group is in fact generated by diagonal, elementary and permutation matrices.

2.2.4

The same methods allow to define a local fundamental class for all \(Y\subset X\) locally defined by \(c\) equations (where fewer equations are enough, the class is null).

2.2.5

One can move from such a local fundamental class, in \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^0\left(Y,\mathsf{R}^{2 c} i^!\mathbb{Z}/n(c)\right)\), to a global fundamental class, in \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_Y^{2 c}\left(X,\mathbb{Z}/n(c)\right)\), when one has semi-pure results:

Proposition 2.2.6

  1. If \(\mathsf{R}^p i^!\mathbb{Z}/n=0\) for \(p<2 c\), then

    \[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_Y^{2 c}\left(X,\mathbb{Z}/n(c)\right) \xrightarrow{\sim} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^0\left(Y,\mathsf{R}^{2 c} i^! \mathbb{Z}/n(c)\right) \text{.} \]

  2. Either \(Z\) a closed part of \(Y\), complement \(V\) in \(Y\), and \(k\) the inclusion of \(Z\) in \(X\). If \(\mathsf{R}^p i^! \mathbb{Z}/n = 0\) for \(p\leqslant 2 c\), then

    \[ \underline{\operatorname{H}}_Y^{2 c}\left(X,\mathbb{Z}/n(c)\right) \hookrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{H}}_V^{2 c}\left(X,\mathbb{Z}/n(c)\right) \text{.} \]

    If \(\mathsf{R}^p i^!\mathbb{Z}/n=0\) for \(p\leqslant 2 c+1\), this arrow is an isomorphism.

The hypothesis \(\mathsf{R}^p i^!\mathbb{Z}/n=0\) is equivalent to \(\mathsf{R}^p i^!\mathbb{Z}/n(c)=0\). That said, (i) is read on the spectral sequence \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^p(Y,\mathsf{R}^q i^!)\Rightarrow \underline{\operatorname{H}}_Y^{p+q}(X,-)\). If \(k_1\) the inclusion of \(Z\) in \(Y\), we have \(k=i k_1\), from where \(\mathsf{R} k^! \mathbb{Z}/n=\mathsf{R} k_1^! \mathsf{R} i^!\), and the exact long sequence of cohomology for \(Z\subset Y\) provides an exact long sequence

\[ \xymatrix{ \cdots \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^i\left(Z,\mathsf{R} k^! \mathbb{Z}/n(c)\right) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}_Y^i\left(X,\mathbb{Z}/n(c)\right) \ar[r] & \underline{\operatorname{H}}_V^i\left(X,\mathbb{Z}/n(c)\right) \ar[r] & \cdots } \]

of which (ii) results.

2.2.7 Amplification

The proposal 2.2.6 remains valid for \(i\) any separated morphism of finite type, and \(2 c\) any integer (positive or negative), provided that it is replaced \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_Y^{2 c}\left(X,\mathbb{Z}/n(c)\right)\) by \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^{2 c}\left(Y,\mathsf{R} i^!\mathbb{Z}/n(c)\right)\), and likewise for \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}_V\).

The following semi-purity results result from: [24]. We will recall their evidence.

Theorem 2.2.8

Or a morphism of \(S\)-schemes of finite type

\[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[r]^-i \ar[dr]_-f & X \ar[d]^-g \\ & S } \]

It is assumed \(X\) smooth, purely of relative dimension \(N\) and that \(Y\) is fiber by fiber of dimension \(\leqslant d\). Or \(c=N-d\).

pages.title: Application: the Lefschetz trace formula in the clean and smooth case
pages.html:
3 Application: Lefschetz trace formula in the clean and smooth case

3.1

It consists of \(X\) and \(Y\) clean and smooth algebraic varieties on an algebraically closed field \(k\), purely of dimensions \(N\) and \(M\). Through the isomorphism of Künneth \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X\times Y)=\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X)\otimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(Y)\), trace morphism \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X\times Y)(M)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X)\) is none other than \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X)\otimes (\)morphism trace \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(Y)(M)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbb{Q}_\ell)\). Since morphism is homogenous of even degree, there is no problem of sign. It is noted \(\int_Y\).

3.2

A class \(\eta\in \underline{\operatorname{H}}^{2 q}(X\times Y)(q)\) defines a degree morphism \(2(q-N)\) \(\eta_{XY}:\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(Y)(N-q)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X)\), by the formula

\[ \beta \mapsto \int_Y \eta\cdot \operatorname{pr}_2^\ast \beta \text{.} \]

Proposition. Be \(p\) and \(q\) two integers, with \(p+q=N+M\), and \(\varepsilon\in \underline{\operatorname{H}}^{2 p}(X\times Y)(p)\), \(\eta\in \underline{\operatorname{H}}^{2 q}(X\times Y)(p)\). The trace of \(\eta_{X Y}\varepsilon_{Y X}:\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X)\) being understood as 1.3, then we have

\[ \operatorname{tr}_{X\times Y}(\eta\cdot \varepsilon) = \operatorname{tr}\left(\eta_{X Y}\varepsilon_{Y X},\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X)\right) \text{.} \]

Proposition.

To exorcise the signs, it is worth remembering from the graduation of \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet\) only the \(\mathbb{Z}/2\)-graduation underlying. To avoid dragging the twists to the Tate with oneself, one is also fixed an isomorphism \(\mathbb{Q}_\ell(1)=\mathbb{Q}_\ell\). Either \(\alpha_Y\) homomorphism \(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(Y)^\vee\) (an isomorphism) making the diagram commutative

\[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(Y)\otimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(Y) \ar[r] \ar[d]^-{\alpha_Y\otimes 1} & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(Y) \ar[r]^-{\operatorname{tr}} & \mathbb{Q}_\ell \ar@{=}[d] \\ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(Y)^\vee \otimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(Y) \ar[rr]^-{\text{(1.3.1)}} & & \mathbb{Q}_\ell . } \]

The application \(\eta_{X Y}\) is the image of \(\eta\) by

\[ \xymatrix{ \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X\times Y) = \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X)\otimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(Y) \ar[r]^-{\alpha_Y} & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X) \otimes \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(Y)^\vee \ar@{=}[r]^-{\text{(1.3.2)}} & \hom\left(\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X),\underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(Y)\right) , } \]

and the same for \(\varepsilon\). The definition 1.3.4 of the trace then brings back 3.3 to the formula \(\operatorname{tr}_{X\times Y}=\operatorname{tr}_X\otimes \operatorname{tr}_Y\).

3.4. Note

Evidence of 3.4 does not use that \(\alpha_X\) and \(\alpha_Y\) are isomorphisms. It is worthless to assume \(X\) and \(Y\) smooth. However, apart from the smooth case, it is difficult to find classes of cohomology to which one wishes to apply 3.3.

3.5 Note

If \(\varepsilon,\eta\) are the cohomology classes of algebraic cycles \(A\) and \(B\) on \(X\times Y\), co-dimension \(p\) and \(q\), and \(A\cap B\) is of dimension \(0\), then \(\operatorname{tr}_{X\times Y}(\eta\varepsilon)\) is the multiplicity of intersection \(A\cdot B\), calculated by an alternate sum of \(\operatorname{Tor}\)(2.3.9).

Proposition. For \(q=M\) and \(\eta=\operatorname{cl}(B)\) the class of a sub-scheme \(B\subset X\times Y\), finished on \(X\), the application \(\eta_{X Y}\) is the compound

\[ \xymatrix{ \eta_{X Y} : \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(Y) \ar[r]^-{\operatorname{pr}_2^\ast} & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(B) \ar[r]^-{\operatorname{tr}_{B/X}} & \underline{\operatorname{H}}^\bullet(X) . } \]

Proposition.

Based on 3.2 and (2.3.3.1) for \(\mathscr{K}=\mathscr{O}_B\), we have in fact

\[ \eta_{X Y}(\beta) = \operatorname{tr}_{X\times Y/X}\left(\operatorname{cl}(B)\cdot \operatorname{pr}_2^\ast\beta\right) = \operatorname{tr}_{B/X}\left(\operatorname{pr}_2^\ast \beta\right) \text{.} \]

The most important case is where \(B\) is the graph of an application \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\). In this case, 3.6 shows that \(\eta_{X Y}=f^\ast\).

corollary_ Either \(f\) an endomorphism of \(X\) clean and smooth on an algebraically closed field \(k\). It is assumed that the fixed points of \(f\) are isolated. So the trace \(\sum (-1)^i \operatorname{tr}(f^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}^i(X))\) is the number of fixed points of \(f\), each counted with its multiplicity. corollary_

In 3.5, we take \(A=\)identity graph, \(B=\)graph of \(f\) and apply 3.3, 3.6.

corollary_ Be \(X\) a curve on \(k\) algebraically closed, deduced by extension of the scalar of \(X_0\) on \(\mathbb{F}_q\), and \(f\) the morphism of Frobenius. So \(\sum (-1)^i \operatorname{tr}\left(f^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(X)\right)\) is the number of fixed points of \(f\). corollary_

We can replace \(X\) by \(X_\text{red}\), so assume \(X\) reduced. Either \(U\) the opening of \(X\) where \(X\) is smooth in size \(1\), and \(\overline{U}\) the projective and smooth completion of \(U\). The exact long cohomology suites for \((X,U)\) and \((\overline{U},U)\) give

\[ \operatorname{tr}\left(f^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(X)\right) = \operatorname{tr}\left(f^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(U)\right) + \operatorname{tr}\left(f^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(X\setminus U)\right) \]

and

\[ \operatorname{tr}\left(f^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(\overline{U})\right) = \operatorname{tr}\left(f^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(U)\right) + \operatorname{tr}\left(f^\ast,\underline{\operatorname{H}}_c^\bullet(\overline{U}\setminus U)\right) \text{.} \]

The same formula applies to fixed point numbers. 3.8 being clear for a dimension diagram \(0\), this brings to prove 3.8 for \(\overline{U}\). This case is justiciable from 3.7. The fact that \(d f=0\) ensures that fixed points are all multiplicity one.

3.9. Note

For \(X\) of dimension \(\leqslant 1\) on \(k\) algebraically closed, and \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) such as \(d f=0\), it is not difficult to verify that 3.8 is still valid.
